title: Evenings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Garfield hits pause on data centers after a sloppy filing sparks bigger questions on power and water.

lines.text: Good evening and welcome to Evenings with Graham & Emmy, the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy. Tonight we're starting with a township that just slammed the brakes on new projects after one incomplete application landed on their desk.

That's right, Emmy. Garfield Township supervisors voted today to block new data centers for at least a year. The move came after a project filing arrived that was missing key details on power lines, water use, and more.
What exactly was sloppy about it? Was it just paperwork or something bigger that got residents worried?

The application was incomplete on several fronts, including how the facility would connect to the grid and draw from local water supplies. Supervisors decided they needed time to write proper rules before anything big locks in.
This feels like part of a bigger pattern we've seen across the state. How many other places have done something similar lately?

Quite a few. Delta County just passed its own one-year ban after residents raised noise and grid concerns. Calhoun County is debating the same thing over power strain and whether jobs will actually benefit locals. Wixom extended its pause, and Williamston Township shelved its zoning rules for now.
Why are so many rural and suburban townships hitting pause at once? Is it the scale of these projects or something specific about Michigan's grid?

It's the combination. A single hyperscale campus can pull hundreds of megawatts and millions of gallons of water daily. That kind of demand hits ratepayers through higher bills or new infrastructure costs, especially in DTE territory where several projects are clustering.
We've heard about DTE's earnings getting a boost from data centers. Does that mean households end up paying more to support the growth?

Often yes. Yesterday's Q2 preview from DTE highlighted data-center demand as a profit driver, but that expansion usually means added charges passed along. Michigan ratepayers have already seen similar costs from earlier projects in the service area.
What about the land itself? Are we talking prime farmland or just empty fields in most of these cases?

It varies, but many proposals sit near residential areas or working farms. In Garfield the site is close enough that neighbors are already asking about property values and long-term water availability.
So the one-year freeze gives the township breathing room to draft rules. What kinds of standards are they likely looking at?

Noise limits, setbacks from homes, water-use caps, and maybe requirements for the company to cover some grid upgrades instead of passing everything to ratepayers. Other townships that paused earlier are using this time for exactly that.
Who are the main players pushing back versus pushing forward in these fights?

Residents and local boards are usually the ones raising red flags on resources and taxes. Companies like Oracle and their supporters argue for jobs and economic development, and we've seen lobbying flip positions, like in Saline Township recently.
That Saline reversal on tax incentives for the Oracle project happened yesterday too. How does that fit into the bigger picture?

It shows the tension. The board restored the company's shot at a big tax break after earlier limits. That could mean forgoing years of property-tax revenue on a multibillion-dollar facility that would still pull heavy power and water from the DTE area.
And Augusta Township is nearing its own vote on zoning and tax terms for a similar-scale project. What happens if that one goes through?
It would add another large load in the same DTE territory as Saline, affecting how much new demand hits ratepayers and whether the township gets meaningful revenue in return.
Down in Cass County, Dowagiac residents are already dealing with noise from an operating facility. How common is that complaint becoming?
It's one of the first real-world examples we're seeing. The humming from cooling systems is disrupting sleep, and people are exploring legal options, including possible class-action steps.
With all these local pauses stacking up, is there any statewide coordination or is every township basically on its own?

Mostly on their own right now. MLive just launched a new map that tracks every active fight by township and county, showing megawatts demanded and tax deals in real time. It helps residents compare what's happening near them to battles elsewhere.
That sounds useful for folks trying to follow the sprawl. What should listeners watch for in the next few weeks?

The outcome of Augusta's vote, whether Calhoun moves forward with its moratorium, and how Garfield actually uses its pause to write rules. Those decisions will set precedents for the next round of proposals.
And if more townships follow suit, could that slow the overall buildout or just push projects to more willing counties?
It could do both. Companies may target places with fewer restrictions, but the cumulative strain on the grid and water resources is becoming harder to ignore statewide.
Let's move to some quick hits from the last day or so. Starting with DTE customers potentially facing bigger bills as data centers boost utility profits.

Right, the Q2 preview showed data-center growth as a key earnings driver. That demand often means higher rates or new costs passed to households, and we've already seen added charges from similar projects.
Next, Calhoun County weighing a year-long halt over jobs and power concerns. Commissioners are debating after hearing about electricity strain and whether economic gains will reach local taxpayers.

A pause there would delay projects drawing hundreds of megawatts and millions of gallons of water. Residents in Battle Creek and surrounding areas would feel it in both future taxes and monthly utility costs.

Augusta Township is close to voting on Oracle-scale rules that would pull power from the same DTE territory as Saline. The outcome decides how much new load hits ratepayers and what revenue the township collects.

Dowagiac in Cass County has residents reporting ongoing noise from an operating data center's cooling systems. Complaints are growing and legal options including class-action steps are being discussed.
Saline Township reversed its earlier limit on incentives for the Oracle campus after lobbying. Residents now face the prospect of forgoing years of property-tax revenue on a huge project.
Delta County voted to ban new data centers for a year after concerns over noise, water, and the rural grid. The moratorium gives time to write permanent zoning rules.
MLive's new map is live and shows every active data center fight across Michigan by township and county, including megawatts and tax deals.
Wixom kept its data center pause in place to shield residents from power strain and noise risks that could raise local costs.
And Williamston Township postponed action on new zoning rules, leaving uncertainty high for nearby homes and farms over noise, power lines, and water use.
On deck for listeners who want to attend meetings, keep an eye on Augusta's upcoming vote on zoning and tax terms for that large proposal.
Calhoun County commissioners are still debating their moratorium, so check local notices for the next public hearing.
Garfield Township will be working on its new rules during the pause, so any upcoming workshops or board sessions there are worth watching.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker. We're back tomorrow.

episode_title: Garfield Township Freezes Data Centers After Sloppy Filing Leaves Residents in Limbo
